Article.ArticleTitle: Response of phytoplankton to near-future multi-pressures in the East China Sea.

Abstract.AbstractText: Shedding light on the response of phytoplankton on continental shelf seas to multi-pressures, is of vital importance for human beings to address future changes. We use a 3-dimension biophysical-geochemical coupled model to investigate the phytoplankton variations of the East China Sea (ECS) in the near-future (2050s). Results indicate that the phytoplankton phenology in the euphotic zone changes greatly off the Zhejiang coastal. The onset of spring bloom shifts to an earlier date in the near-future relative to the 2010s, especially under the high emission scenario (∼2 weeks), and an increase of 1 °C in water temperature could lead to five days in advance of the bloom onset. Despite the prolonging growing season, the near-future phytoplankton biomass in spring is close to the contemporary level, owing to the climate-induced peak clipping. Additionally, phytoplankton succession from diatom to non-diatom also arrives earlier, which is in association with the intensified riverine nutrient inputs from Changjiang and the heat flux changes. In summer, both the elevated Changjiang inputs and climate change promote the growth of phytoplankton in the euphotic zone, and among climate changes, the southwesterly wind anormaly in summer over the ECS plays a key role. These findings underscore phenology changes under the near-future multi-pressures off coastal waters, long-term observation for phytoplankton should be taken into consideration.
